content:
You open a brand-new backend repo, pull down the latest changes, and open package.json. There it is, sitting near the top:
"dependencies": {
"express": "^4.21.0"
}You take a deep breath. It’s 2026. Every developer on YouTube is babbling about Fastify’s insane benchmarks, NestJS’s clean architecture, and Hono running on edge networks in microseconds. Yet here we are, about to build another backend service using a framework that was created when Obama, was in his first term.
Why are we all still using Express when practically every modern alternative is objectively better engineered on paper?
It is exhausting to feel like you are falling behind on modern tooling, only to have your team agree on choosing tools from a decade ago. But if we want to move past the hype of the modern alternatives, we have to understand the paradox: why does the industry refuse to let Express die?
Imagine you are starting a new microservice. You want to build things the “right” way — according to the industry standard. You want automatic request validation, fast route matching, etc.
You look at the modern ecosystem of backend web frameworks, and the options look incredible:

• Fastify: Built-in JSON schema validation that stops bad requests before they ever touch your route logic, plus 5 times the throughput of Express.

• NestJS: Angular-style dependency injection and decorators that give massive enterprise applications actual structure.

• Hono: A ultrafast, lightweight router that runs identically on Node, Bun, Deno, or Cloudflare Workers without touching a single config file.
Fired up, you write a pitch, drop it in your team’s Slack channel. You present the benchmarks. You show how clean the code looks.

Get Shreyash’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
Remember me for faster sign in

Your tech lead listens, nods thoughtfully, and then says: “Appreciate the research, but let me save you some time: we’re just going to use Express.”
It feels like a door slamming shut. You leave the meeting feeling like your team is adamant, choosing to live in the past while the rest of the web moves forward.
Why Express Feels So Ancient
Let’s be honest about why Express frustrates us in the first place:
1. It Was Built for the Callback Era
Express was designed back when JavaScript didn’t even have native Promise support, let alone async/await. Because of that legacy core, handling asynchronous errors in older Express setups required wrapping every single route in an asyncHandler wrapper just to keep uncaught promise rejections from crashing your entire server process.
2. It Has Zero Opinion on Architecture
Express doesn’t care how you structure your folders, how you validate incoming JSON, or how you connect to your database. For a developer looking for best practices, initializing an empty Express app doesn’t feel like freedom — it feels like being dropped in the middle of the ocean without a life jacket and being told to “just figure it out.”
3. It Evolves at a Glacier’s Pace
While the rest of the JavaScript world moves at breakneck speed, Express barely changes. Major version updates take years because breaking a single core pattern would fracture half the backend infrastructure on the internet.
So if Express is slow to evolve, lacks built-in TypeScript magic, and gets destroyed in every best backend web frameworks to use debates on Twitter… why haven’t we got rid of it yet?

If you’re ready to find out the reason Express refuses to die — and why sticking with it might actually be the smartest move your team can make — check out the upcoming Part 2.
